Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Living the Link Pte Ltd (in creditors’ voluntary liquidation)

Facts.Text: Living the Link Pte Ltd was part of the Link Group of companies involved in high end retail fashion in Singapore.
Living the Link Pte Ltd was incorporated to carry on a fashion and lifestyle concept store business under the name "Living the Link".
Living the Link Pte Ltd entered into a three-year tenancy agreement for premises at 1 Nassim Road with Cheong’s Company Pte Ltd.
The tenancy for 1 Nassim Road between Living the Link Pte Ltd and Cheong’s Company Pte Ltd was extended to 31 March 2011.
Living the Link Pte Ltd opened a store at 1 Nassim Road in the second half of 2007 to operate a fashion and lifestyle concept store combining high fashion retail with food and beverage outlets.
By 10 January 2008, Cheong’s Company Pte Ltd had issued demands to Living the Link Pte Ltd for outstanding rental, and Living the Link Pte Ltd was in rental arrears during 2008.
Throughout 2008, Living the Link Pte Ltd experienced cash-flow difficulties and was unable to pay all debts, including rental, as they fell due without financial support from related companies.

On 24 October 2008, Living the Link Pte Ltd wrote to Cheong’s Company Pte Ltd’s property agent requesting a waiver of two months’ rental and a reduction of rental, stating that sales at 1 Nassim Road were lower than at its previous location in the Mandarin Hotel.

On 25 November 2008, a S$2,000,000 credit facility with the Bank of East Asia that had been used by Link Boutique Pte Ltd was transferred so that borrowings would be booked under Living the Link Pte Ltd, with drawdowns first to pay outstanding sums owed by Link Boutique Pte Ltd and Alldressedup International Pte Ltd to the bank.
As at 31 December 2008, the audited accounts of Living the Link Pte Ltd recorded no inventory on its books.

On 31 December 2008, Living the Link Pte Ltd issued debit notes recording transfers of inventory with a book value of S$1,289,858.90 to Link Boutique Pte Ltd and S$1,337,926.50 to Alldressedup International Pte Ltd.
On 31 December 2008, Living the Link Pte Ltd recorded transfers of fixed assets to Link Boutique Pte Ltd in its accounts.

Between 13 May 2008 and 31 December 2008, Living the Link Pte Ltd made cash transfers totalling S$3,354,174.35 to Link Boutique Pte Ltd and received S$469,000 from Link Boutique Pte Ltd, resulting in a net cash outflow of S$2,885,174.35 from Living the Link Pte Ltd to Link Boutique Pte Ltd.
In December 2008, Living the Link Pte Ltd made 23 cash payments totalling S$361,050 to Link Boutique Pte Ltd and received one payment of S$41,000 from Link Boutique Pte Ltd.

During 2008, Link Boutique Pte Ltd supplied inventory worth approximately S$2.9 million and paid operating expenses of about S$1.3 million on behalf of Living the Link Pte Ltd, which were recorded via debit notes.
From January 2009, sales at the 1 Nassim Road premises were carried out using inventory owned by Link Boutique Pte Ltd, with related expenses charged to Link Boutique Pte Ltd.

Between January and August 2009, Link Boutique Pte Ltd transferred S$903,000 and Alldressedup International Pte Ltd transferred S$125,500 to Living the Link Pte Ltd, and Living the Link Pte Ltd used these funds to pay rental and wage or staff costs that were recorded as expenses of Link Boutique Pte Ltd and other related entities.
From January 2009 to July 2009, wage and staff costs of S$762,073.38 related to operations at 1 Nassim Road and other group entities were charged to Link Boutique Pte Ltd.

From January 2009 to July 2009, the net monthly rental for the 1 Nassim Road premises, after deducting sub-tenant rental, was S$41,840.53, resulting in total net rental of S$292,883.71 for that period.
On 1 April 2009, Living the Link Pte Ltd transferred 120,000 ordinary shares in Graha Lifestyle Pte Ltd, recorded at a book value of S$120,000, to Link Boutique Pte Ltd.
On 29 July 2009, Living the Link Pte Ltd offered to settle the July 2009 rental if Cheong’s Company Pte Ltd accepted a mutual termination of the tenancy agreement.
On 31 July 2009, Living the Link Pte Ltd closed its business at 1 Nassim Road and terminated the lease for the premises.
Between 1 May 2008 and 15 July 2009, Living the Link Pte Ltd paid personal expenses of Tan Lay Tin Tina and her husband Lionel Leo amounting to S$41,738.80.
In a Statement of Affairs dated 31 March 2010, Cheong’s Company Pte Ltd was listed as a creditor of Living the Link Pte Ltd.
On 13 May 2010, a creditors’ meeting for Living the Link Pte Ltd was held and a resolution for creditors’ voluntary liquidation was passed.
On 13 May 2010, Living the Link Pte Ltd was placed in creditors’ voluntary liquidation.

Cheong’s Company Pte Ltd filed a Proof of Debt against Living the Link Pte Ltd in the sum of S$1,297,135.75, comprising rental arrears, damages for lost rental, damages from reduced rental, reinstatement costs, legal costs, agents’ fees, and deductions for part payments, replacement tenant rental, and deposit set-off.

The initial liquidators of Living the Link Pte Ltd were replaced pursuant to an application by Cheong’s Company Pte Ltd, which funded the subsequent claim against Tan Lay Tin Tina and the associate companies.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether pre-liquidation transfers of inventory, shares, and cash from Living the Link Pte Ltd to related companies, and payments of personal expenses, altered the distribution of assets between related and unrelated creditors at a time when Living the Link Pte Ltd was unable to meet its obligations as they fell due.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a company is wound up, transactions occurring within a specified period before the commencement of winding up that place certain creditors in a better position than others may be set aside if statutory conditions on timing, insolvency, and preferential effect are met, and directors of a company in financial difficulty are manage the company’s assets with regard to the interests of its creditors as a whole.
Party_Details.Application: Living the Link Pte Ltd operated a fashion and lifestyle business at 1 Nassim Road under a tenancy that extended to 31 March 2011, and from early 2008 it experienced persistent rental arrears and cash-flow difficulties while relying on financial support from related companies. In the two years before its creditors’ voluntary liquidation on 13 May 2010, Living the Link Pte Ltd recorded substantial cash transfers to Link Boutique Pte Ltd, transferred inventory at book value to Link Boutique Pte Ltd and Alldressedup International Pte Ltd, and transferred shares in Graha Lifestyle Pte Ltd to Link Boutique Pte Ltd, with corresponding reductions in intercompany balances. During the same period, Cheong’s Company Pte Ltd was pressing for rental payments and was later listed as a creditor in the Statement of Affairs, and Living the Link Pte Ltd closed its business and terminated the lease in July 2009. These events , before liquidation, assets and saleable stock originally held by Living the Link Pte Ltd were moved to related entities while a substantial unrelated landlord claim remained outstanding.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed the statutory framework under section 329 of the Companies Act read with sections 99, 100, and 102 of the Bankruptcy Act, and accepted that the impugned transactions occurred within the relevant two-year period and had the effect of placing the related companies in a better position in the event of liquidation. It held that Living the Link Pte Ltd was insolvent on a cash-flow basis from January 2008, and also insolvent on a balance sheet basis by 31 December 2008 once prospective rental liabilities were taken into account. The court rejected the defendants’ attempt to shift the inventory transfer date from 31 December 2008 to April 2009, relying on audited accounts, monthly balance sheets, debit notes, internal emails, and prior examination transcripts to conclude that the inventory transfers occurred on 31 December 2008 and that Living the Link Pte Ltd effectively ceased its retail business thereafter. The court found that the statutory presumption of a desire to prefer under section 99(5) of the Bankruptcy Act applied because Link Boutique Pte Ltd and Alldressedup International Pte Ltd were associates, and held that the defendants had not rebutted this presumption in relation to the inventory and Graha share transfers. It rejected the contention that Tan Lay Tin Tina genuinely believed Cheong’s Company Pte Ltd would not be a creditor, pointing to the absence of an express contractual obligation on Cheong’s part to provide the specific power supply, the late raising of the power supply issue, the earlier rental waiver request letter that did not mention misrepresentation or repudiatory breach, the listing of Cheong as a creditor in the Statement of Affairs and meeting notice, and the offer of mutual termination with payment of July rental. The court also found that the 2009 bank transfers from Link Boutique Pte Ltd and Alldressedup International Pte Ltd to Living the Link Pte Ltd were used to pay rental and wage or staff costs that were booked to Link Boutique Pte Ltd and other group entities, and therefore did not constitute continued financial support for Living the Link Pte Ltd. For the 2008 cash transfers, the court applied the running account principle as articulated in Airservices Australia and Progen, and concluded that, for most of 2008, the payments formed part of a continuing business relationship in which Link Boutique Pte Ltd supplied inventory and paid operating expenses while Living the Link Pte Ltd remitted sale proceeds, so those payments were made for commercial reasons rather than to prefer Link Boutique Pte Ltd. However, for the 23 payments in December 2008 totalling S$361,050, the court held that the defendants had not shown that these payments were made with the intention of obtaining new value, given that the decision to wind down Living the Link Pte Ltd had been taken by then and there was insufficient evidence linking those payments to further supplies or support; accordingly, the presumption of desire to prefer was not rebutted for those payments. The court therefore set aside as undue preferences the inventory transfers to Link Boutique Pte Ltd and Alldressedup International Pte Ltd on 31 December 2008, the transfer of Graha Lifestyle Pte Ltd shares to Link Boutique Pte Ltd on 1 April 2009, and the December 2008 cash payments from Living the Link Pte Ltd to Link Boutique Pte Ltd. In determining the quantum to be restored, the court started from the aggregate book value of the transferred inventory of S$2,627,785.40 rather than the recorded sale proceeds, because the defendants could not account for unsold and obsolete stock and the sold stock had generated profits. It then deducted net rental of S$292,883.71 and wage or staff costs of S$762,073.38 for January to July 2009, totalling S$1,054,957.09, on the basis that these expenses would have been incurred by Living the Link Pte Ltd if it had retained and sold the inventory. The court allocated the deductions proportionately between Link Boutique Pte Ltd and Alldressedup International Pte Ltd, resulting in repayment obligations of S$772,029.02 and S$800,799.29 respectively for the inventory, plus S$120,000 for the Graha shares and S$361,050 for the December 2008 cash payments, for a total of S$2,053,878.31 to be repaid to Living the Link Pte Ltd. The court considered whether it had power under section 99(2) of the Bankruptcy Act to order only a partial reversal of the undue preferences sufficient to satisfy the claims of Cheong’s Company Pte Ltd and other unrelated creditors, and concluded that the statutory language "as it thinks fit" conferred a broad discretion that could, in an appropriate case, support a partial reversal, drawing analogies with remedies for transactions at undervalue and English authorities such as Paramount Airways and Pena. It noted, however, that a partial reversal would require the court to project and effectively pre-empt the liquidators’ adjudication of proofs of debt and to estimate liquidation expenses, and that in this case the parties had not agreed on a common base figure or on amounts to be reserved for costs. The court therefore declined to craft a partial reversal on the facts and ordered full reversal of the identified undue preferences, while inviting the parties to agree on a lesser sum if they wished. On directors’ duties, the court applied Progen and held that once a company is insolvent or nearly insolvent, directors must consider creditors’ interests and ensure that assets are not applied in a way that prejudices those interests. It reasoned that the same facts that established undue preferences also demonstrated that Tan Lay Tin Tina had caused company assets to be applied in a way that favoured related creditors and improved the position of entities whose bank facilities she had personally guaranteed, thereby breaching her fiduciary duties. The court rejected an application to excuse her under section 391 of the Companies Act, finding that factors such as partial rental payments, satisfaction of other creditors’ claims, and her personal financial losses and reputational impact did not show that she had acted honestly and reasonably in relation to the impugned transactions. In considering remedies against her personally, the court reviewed English authorities including West Mercia and Hellard, which treat a director who procures a preferential payment as liable to restore the misapplied funds to the company, even where the payment reduces a genuine company debt and even if the director does not receive the funds personally. The court accepted the reasoning that such liability is analogous to that of a trustee required to restore a misapplied fund and that proof of net loss to the company is not a prerequisite. It concluded that an order requiring Tan Lay Tin Tina to pay to Living the Link Pte Ltd a sum equal to the value of the undue preference transactions, coupled with appropriate adjustments to the related companies’ claims in the liquidation, was an appropriate means of enforcing her duties and aligning the outcome with the statutory avoidance regime. The court’s overall conclusion was that the impugned inventory transfers, the Graha share transfer, and the December 2008 cash payments were undue preferences, that the related companies must restore S$2,053,878.31 to Living the Link Pte Ltd, and that Tan Lay Tin Tina was personally in breach of her duties and liable in a corresponding amount, with the detailed mechanics of distribution and claim adjustments to be implemented within the liquidation process.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Chia Soo Hien
Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-liquidation transfers of assets and payments by Living the Link Pte Ltd to related companies affected the recovery prospects of unsecured creditors represented by the liquidators.
Party_Details.Rule: In insolvency, transactions that place certain creditors in a better position than others within a defined look-back period may be adjusted so that unsecured creditors share available assets according to the statutory priority scheme.
Party_Details.Application: As a plaintiff acting through the liquidators of Living the Link Pte Ltd, Chia Soo Hien’s position depended on whether the court would unwind transfers of inventory, shares, and cash to related companies so that more assets would be available for distribution to unsecured creditors.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court treated the plaintiffs collectively as the liquidators of Living the Link Pte Ltd and focused on whether the challenged transactions were avoidable as undue preferences. Having found that certain transfers of inventory, shares, and cash were undue preferences and ordering restoration of value to the company, the court effectively granted the relief sought by the plaintiffs to increase the pool of assets available for unsecured creditors.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Leow Quek Shiong
Party_Details.Issue: Whether the impugned transactions reduced the assets available to Living the Link Pte Ltd’s unsecured creditors and should be reversed for the benefit of those creditors.
Party_Details.Rule: Liquidators may seek to set aside transactions that meet statutory criteria for avoidance so that the company’s estate is restored for collective distribution among unsecured creditors.
Party_Details.Application: As one of the plaintiffs associated with the liquidators, Leow Quek Shiong’s interest aligned with the effort to restore value to Living the Link Pte Ltd by challenging transfers to related companies that occurred when the company was in financial difficulty.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court accepted the liquidators’ core contention that certain transfers of inventory, shares, and cash from Living the Link Pte Ltd to related companies during the relevant period should be unwound as undue preferences, thereby increasing the estate available for unsecured creditors represented by the plaintiffs.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Tan Lay Tin Tina

Facts.Text: In 1982, Tan Lay Tin Tina founded Link Boutique Pte Ltd as a retailer of ladies’ apparel and high fashion merchandise in Singapore.
In early 2005, Tan Lay Tin Tina began to design and produce fashion wear under Link Boutique Pte Ltd’s own label "alldressedup".
In 2005, Alldressedup International Pte Ltd was incorporated to carry on the production and sale of the "alldressedup" label associated with Link Boutique Pte Ltd.
On 9 January 2007, Living the Link Pte Ltd was incorporated to carry on a fashion and lifestyle concept store business conceptualised by Tan Lay Tin Tina.
In the third quarter of 2006, the Link Group had to relocate its flagship store from the Mandarin Hotel after receiving notice to vacate due to planned renovations.
Around the third quarter of 2006, Tan Lay Tin Tina identified 1 Nassim Road as a suitable location for a new flagship store near Orchard Road.
Around 2006, Tan Lay Tin Tina conceptualised a fashion and lifestyle concept store at 1 Nassim Road combining high fashion retail with food and beverage outlets under the name "Living the Link".

At all material times, Tan Lay Tin Tina was a director and the sole shareholder of Living the Link Pte Ltd, Link Boutique Pte Ltd, and Alldressedup International Pte Ltd through their parent company Fashionation International Pte Ltd.
During 2008, Lionel Leo, the husband of Tan Lay Tin Tina, was the CEO of the Link Group.
In 2008, Link Boutique Pte Ltd used its banking facilities to purchase inventory for Living the Link Pte Ltd, and repayments to the banks were made by Link Boutique Pte Ltd.

In 2008, banks pressed Link Boutique Pte Ltd and Alldressedup International Pte Ltd to settle outstanding loans amounting to over S$1.5 million, for which Tan Lay Tin Tina had provided personal guarantees.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before the commencement of liquidation, Tan Lay Tin Tina caused Living the Link Pte Ltd to enter into transactions with related companies that altered the position of creditors and whether her conduct as director in relation to those transactions triggered personal financial responsibility.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors who control a company in financial difficulty are manage its affairs with regard to the collective interests of creditors, and where they cause the company to enter into transactions that are later set aside under insolvency avoidance provisions, courts may require them to restore to the company the value of assets or payments that were redirected.
Party_Details.Application: Tan Lay Tin Tina controlled Living the Link Pte Ltd, Link Boutique Pte Ltd, and Alldressedup International Pte Ltd through a common holding structure and directed the relocation of the group’s flagship retail operations to 1 Nassim Road under the "Living the Link" concept. While Living the Link Pte Ltd faced persistent rental arrears and cash-flow constraints in 2008 and 2009, she oversaw arrangements under which inventory and fixed assets recorded in Living the Link Pte Ltd’s accounts were transferred to Link Boutique Pte Ltd and Alldressedup International Pte Ltd, and cash flows from Living the Link Pte Ltd were regularly remitted to Link Boutique Pte Ltd in the context of group financing and bank facilities that she had personally guaranteed. These steps shifted stock and value from Living the Link Pte Ltd to related entities while a substantial landlord claim remained outstanding, and the court treated her as the directing mind responsible for the company’s participation in the transactions that were later set aside.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court identified Tan Lay Tin Tina as the directing mind and sole shareholder of Living the Link Pte Ltd and its associate companies, and applied the principle from Progen that directors of an insolvent or near-insolvent company must consider creditors’ interests and avoid applying assets in a way that prejudices those interests. Having found that the inventory transfers, the Graha share transfer, and the December 2008 cash payments were undue preferences in favour of related companies, the court inferred that she had misapplied company assets to the detriment of unsecured creditors. It rejected her explanation that she believed Cheong’s Company Pte Ltd would not be a creditor, pointing to the contractual documents, the timing of complaints about power supply, the rental waiver request letter, the listing of Cheong as a creditor, and the mutual termination offer. The court also noted that the proceeds of the impugned transfers were used mainly to reduce bank loans of Link Boutique Pte Ltd and Alldressedup International Pte Ltd that were secured by her personal guarantees, and concluded that she improved her own position as guarantor through these transactions. The court declined to excuse her under section 391 of the Companies Act, finding that she had not acted honestly and reasonably in procuring the transactions. Relying on English authorities such as West Mercia and Hellard, the court held that a director who causes a company to make a preferential payment can be ordered to restore to the company the value of the misapplied funds, even if the payment reduces a genuine company debt and even if the director does not receive the funds directly. It treated her liability as analogous to that of a fiduciary required to reconstitute a misapplied fund and concluded that she should be personally liable in an amount corresponding to the value of the undue preference transactions, with appropriate adjustments to the related companies’ claims in the liquidation to avoid double recovery.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Alldressedup International Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Alldressedup International Pte Ltd was incorporated in 2005 to carry on the production and sale of the "alldressedup" fashion label associated with Link Boutique Pte Ltd.
Alldressedup International Pte Ltd was an associate company of Living the Link Pte Ltd within the Link Group and was under the control of Tan Lay Tin Tina through Fashionation International Pte Ltd.
On 31 December 2008, Living the Link Pte Ltd recorded a transfer of inventory with a book value of S$1,337,926.50 to Alldressedup International Pte Ltd via debit notes.

Between January and August 2009, Alldressedup International Pte Ltd transferred S$125,500 to Living the Link Pte Ltd, and Living the Link Pte Ltd used these funds to pay expenses that were recorded as costs of Link Boutique Pte Ltd and other group entities.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether inventory transferred from Living the Link Pte Ltd to Alldressedup International Pte Ltd before liquidation should be restored so that unsecured creditors of Living the Link Pte Ltd are not disadvantaged relative to this related company.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a company in financial difficulty transfers assets to an associate within a statutory look-back period and the transfer is set aside under avoidance provisions, the associate may be restore to the company the value of the assets, adjusted for expenses that would have been incurred if the company had retained and sold the assets.
Party_Details.Application: Alldressedup International Pte Ltd received inventory from Living the Link Pte Ltd at book value on 31 December 2008 while Living the Link Pte Ltd was experiencing cash-flow problems and had a substantial landlord exposure. The court treated this transfer as part of the set of transactions that improved the position of related companies relative to unsecured creditors. In quantifying restoration, the court started from the book value of the inventory transferred to Alldressedup International Pte Ltd and allocated a share of the rental and wage or staff costs that would have been incurred if Living the Link Pte Ltd had retained and sold the inventory, resulting in a net amount to be repaid by Alldressedup International Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that Alldressedup International Pte Ltd was an associate of Living the Link Pte Ltd and that the inventory transfer to it on 31 December 2008 fell within the statutory presumption of desire to prefer. It found no evidence that the transfer was motivated solely by proper commercial considerations unrelated to the relative positions of creditors, and therefore treated the transfer as an undue preference. In determining the remedy, the court used the aggregate book value of all transferred inventory and deducted net rental and wage or staff costs for January to July 2009, then apportioned the resulting net figure between Link Boutique Pte Ltd and Alldressedup International Pte Ltd in proportion to the book value of inventory each received. On this basis, the court ordered Alldressedup International Pte Ltd to repay S$800,799.29 to Living the Link Pte Ltd as part of the overall restoration of S$2,053,878.31.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Link Boutique Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Link Boutique Pte Ltd was founded in 1982 as a retailer of ladies’ apparel and high fashion merchandise in Singapore.
Link Boutique Pte Ltd was part of the Link Group and was associated with Living the Link Pte Ltd and Alldressedup International Pte Ltd under common control by Tan Lay Tin Tina.

In 2008, Link Boutique Pte Ltd supplied inventory worth approximately S$2.9 million and paid about S$1.3 million of operating expenses on behalf of Living the Link Pte Ltd, which were recorded via debit notes.

Between 13 May 2008 and 31 December 2008, Link Boutique Pte Ltd received S$3,354,174.35 in cash transfers from Living the Link Pte Ltd and transferred S$469,000 to Living the Link Pte Ltd, resulting in a net cash inflow of S$2,885,174.35 from Living the Link Pte Ltd.
In December 2008, Link Boutique Pte Ltd received 23 cash payments totalling S$361,050 from Living the Link Pte Ltd and made one payment of S$41,000 to Living the Link Pte Ltd.
On 31 December 2008, Living the Link Pte Ltd recorded a transfer of inventory with a book value of S$1,289,858.90 to Link Boutique Pte Ltd via debit notes.
On 31 December 2008, Living the Link Pte Ltd recorded a transfer of fixed assets to Link Boutique Pte Ltd in its accounts.

From January 2009, sales at the 1 Nassim Road premises were carried out using inventory owned by Link Boutique Pte Ltd, and rental and wage or staff costs for 2009 were debited to Link Boutique Pte Ltd.

Between January and August 2009, Link Boutique Pte Ltd transferred S$903,000 to Living the Link Pte Ltd, and Living the Link Pte Ltd used these funds to pay rental and wage or staff costs that were recorded as expenses of Link Boutique Pte Ltd and other group entities.
On 1 April 2009, Link Boutique Pte Ltd received 120,000 ordinary shares in Graha Lifestyle Pte Ltd from Living the Link Pte Ltd at a recorded book value of S$120,000.

By April 2011, Link Boutique Pte Ltd’s report on the transferred inventory from Living the Link Pte Ltd showed sale proceeds of S$2,035,595, including profits of S$90,114.76, unsold stock with a book value of S$682,335.12, and obsolete stock amounting to S$318,226.59.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether cash, inventory, and shares received by Link Boutique Pte Ltd from Living the Link Pte Ltd before liquidation should be restored, in whole or in part, so that Link Boutique Pte Ltd does not retain a preferential position over other unsecured creditors of Living the Link Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: If a company in financial difficulty transfers cash or assets to an associate within a statutory look-back period and those transfers are set aside as undue preferences, the associate may be repay cash and restore the value of assets received, adjustments for expenses that would have been incurred if the company had retained and realised the assets.
Party_Details.Application: Link Boutique Pte Ltd was a key trading and financing entity within the Link Group and received substantial cash transfers, inventory, fixed assets, and Graha Lifestyle Pte Ltd shares from Living the Link Pte Ltd during the two years before Living the Link Pte Ltd’s creditors’ voluntary liquidation. While Link Boutique Pte Ltd supplied inventory and paid operating expenses for Living the Link Pte Ltd under a running account arrangement, the court distinguished between payments that formed part of ongoing mutual dealings and those made after the decision to wind down Living the Link Pte Ltd’s business. It treated the December 2008 cash payments, the 31 December 2008 inventory transfer, and the 1 April 2009 Graha share transfer as transactions that left Link Boutique Pte Ltd in a better position than other unsecured creditors. In quantifying restoration, the court started from the book value of inventory transferred to Link Boutique Pte Ltd, deducted a proportionate share of net rental and wage or staff costs that would have been incurred if Living the Link Pte Ltd had retained and sold the inventory, and added the book value of the Graha shares and the December 2008 cash payments to determine the total amount Link Boutique Pte Ltd should repay.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court accepted that, for much of 2008, cash transfers between Living the Link Pte Ltd and Link Boutique Pte Ltd occurred under a running account in which Link Boutique Pte Ltd supplied inventory and paid operating expenses while Living the Link Pte Ltd remitted sale proceeds, and held that these payments were made for commercial reasons rather than to prefer Link Boutique Pte Ltd. However, it found that by December 2008 the decision had been taken to wind down Living the Link Pte Ltd’s business, that inventory and fixed assets were transferred to Link Boutique Pte Ltd on 31 December 2008, and that the 23 cash payments in December 2008 totalling S$361,050 could not be linked to further supplies or support. The court therefore held that the statutory presumption of desire to prefer was not rebutted for the December 2008 cash payments, the 31 December 2008 inventory transfer, or the 1 April 2009 Graha share transfer. In determining the remedy, the court used the aggregate book value of all transferred inventory (S$2,627,785.40) rather than the recorded sale proceeds, because Link Boutique Pte Ltd could not satisfactorily account for unsold and obsolete stock and the sold stock had generated profits. It deducted net rental of S$292,883.71 and wage or staff costs of S$762,073.38 for January to July 2009, totalling S$1,054,957.09, on the basis that these expenses would have been incurred by Living the Link Pte Ltd if it had retained and sold the inventory. The court then apportioned the remaining S$1,572,828.31 between Link Boutique Pte Ltd and Alldressedup International Pte Ltd in proportion to the book value of inventory each received, resulting in a repayment obligation of S$772,029.02 for Link Boutique Pte Ltd in respect of inventory. Adding S$120,000 for the Graha shares and S$361,050 for the December 2008 cash payments, the court ordered Link Boutique Pte Ltd to repay a total of S$1,253,079.02 to Living the Link Pte Ltd. The court considered but did not implement a partial reversal limited to the amount needed to satisfy unrelated creditors, noting that such an order would require it to anticipate the liquidators’ adjudication of proofs of debt and liquidation expenses. It concluded that a full reversal of the identified undue preferences, with Link Boutique Pte Ltd’s resulting claims to be addressed in the liquidation process, was the appropriate outcome on the facts.
